clinicalStem: A 14-year-old girl with known tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC) presents to clinic for routine follow-up. She has had well-controlled epilepsy on valproate for four years. Renal ultrasound ordered as part of her surveillance shows multiple bilateral echogenic masses, the largest measuring 4.2 cm in the right kidney. She is currently asymptomatic with normal blood pressure and normal renal function. Which of the following statements BEST describes the next step in her management?
options: Reassure and repeat ultrasound in 3 years as the lesions are incidental findings
Refer for urgent nephrectomy of the right kidney to prevent malignant transformation
Initiate an mTOR inhibitor (everolimus) and arrange MRI to further characterize the lesions
Perform urgent renal biopsy to exclude renal cell carcinoma
Switch antiepileptic to topiramate, which also reduces angiomyolipoma burden
explanation: Angiomyolipomas (AMLs) greater than 3 cm in TSC carry significant risk of spontaneous haemorrhage (Wunderlich syndrome). Current guidelines recommend mTOR inhibitor therapy (everolimus) for growing or large AMLs in TSC, which reduces tumour size and prevents haemorrhagic complications. MRI is preferred over ultrasound for lesion characterisation to exclude fat-poor AML or RCC.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Lesions >3 cm are not safely observed; haemorrhage risk is substantial and warrants active intervention.
Nephrectomy is not first-line; nephron-sparing approaches and mTOR inhibitors are preferred to preserve renal function in a young patient with bilateral disease.
Correct. Everolimus is the evidence-based mTOR inhibitor indicated for TSC-associated AMLs >3 cm; MRI better delineates the lesions.
Biopsy is not routine; AML is a clinical/radiological diagnosis in TSC and biopsy risks haemorrhage from these vascular lesions.
Topiramate has no direct anti-tumour effect on AMLs; antiepileptic choice should not be guided by renal lesion management.
clinicalPearl: In TSC, renal angiomyolipomas >3 cm warrant mTOR inhibitor therapy (everolimus/sirolimus) to reduce haemorrhage risk and shrink lesion volume.
keyClue: AML measuring 4.2 cm (>3 cm threshold) in a TSC patient on routine surveillance.
commonExamTrap: Assuming AMLs in TSC are always benign and require no intervention until symptomatic.
missedConcept: The 3 cm size threshold for active management of TSC-associated AMLs and the role of mTOR inhibitors.

topic: Neurofibromatosis type 1 — optic pathway glioma follow-up
clinicalStem: A 5-year-old boy diagnosed with neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) has been followed in a multidisciplinary clinic since age 2. He has six café-au-lait macules, axillary freckling, and two Lisch nodules on slit-lamp examination. At his annual review, his mother reports he has started sitting closer to the television. Formal visual acuity testing shows 6/18 in the left eye and 6/6 in the right eye. MRI brain and orbits is ordered. Which finding on MRI would be MOST consistent with the likely diagnosis causing his visual deterioration?
options: Enhancing mass in the pineal region with obstructive hydrocephalus
T2-hyperintense fusiform enlargement of the left optic nerve extending to the chiasm
Bilateral temporal lobe signal change consistent with hamartomas
Focal cortical dysplasia in the right occipital lobe
Diffuse leptomeningeal enhancement suggesting carcinomatous meningitis
explanation: Optic pathway gliomas (OPGs) occur in approximately 15% of NF1 patients and are the most common cause of progressive visual loss in this group. They appear on MRI as T2-hyperintense fusiform enlargement of the optic nerve(s), chiasm, or optic tracts. Unilateral visual acuity loss in a child with NF1 is OPG until proven otherwise.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Pineal germinomas are not an NF1-associated tumour; this presentation does not fit the demographic or syndrome.
Correct. T2-hyperintense fusiform enlargement of the optic nerve is the hallmark MRI appearance of OPG in NF1.
Temporal hamartomas do not cause monocular visual loss and are not the primary NF1-associated intracranial lesion causing this presentation.
Focal cortical dysplasia in the occipital lobe would cause visual field defects rather than reduced acuity, and is not the expected NF1 complication here.
Leptomeningeal carcinomatosis is not an NF1 feature in a 5-year-old without prior malignancy.
clinicalPearl: Any NF1 child with declining visual acuity or strabismus needs urgent MRI to exclude optic pathway glioma — annual ophthalmological surveillance is mandatory in NF1.
keyClue: NF1 child with asymmetric visual acuity loss (6/18 left eye) prompting MRI — classic OPG scenario.
commonExamTrap: Attributing the visual change to amblyopia and delaying imaging in an NF1 patient.
missedConcept: Optic pathway glioma is the most common intracranial tumour in NF1 and a major cause of progressive visual morbidity.

clinicalStem: A 4-year-old girl with spastic bilateral cerebral palsy (GMFCS level III) attends a developmental paediatrics clinic. She walks with a walker but tires quickly. Her parents report she has become increasingly irritable and is crying during nappy changes over the past two months. Examination reveals limited hip abduction bilaterally (left >right) and pain on passive left hip movement. Which investigation should be performed FIRST to evaluate the most likely complication?
options: MRI spine to exclude tethered cord syndrome
Plain X-ray of the pelvis including both hips (AP view)
Bone scan to exclude avascular necrosis of the femoral head
Hip ultrasound for developmental dysplasia assessment
Serum inflammatory markers and blood cultures to exclude septic arthritis
explanation: Children with spastic CP, especially those at GMFCS level III–V, are at high risk of progressive hip migration and dislocation due to muscle imbalance. Painful hip in a CP child with limited abduction is hip displacement until proven otherwise. AP pelvis X-ray is the recommended first-line investigation per hip surveillance programmes, allowing calculation of the migration percentage (Reimer's index).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Tethered cord presents with progressive neurological deterioration, bladder dysfunction, and back pain — not isolated hip pain and restricted abduction.
Correct. AP pelvis X-ray is the gold-standard first investigation, enabling measurement of migration percentage to guide further management.
AVN is unlikely as the primary diagnosis here without prior steroid use or trauma history; plain X-ray precedes specialist imaging.
Ultrasound is used in infants <6 months; X-ray is standard from 6 months onward and better assesses migration in CP.
Septic arthritis is possible but the gradual onset over 2 months and bilateral restricted abduction in a CP child make hip dislocation much more likely; X-ray is still the appropriate first step.
clinicalPearl: All children with spastic bilateral CP (GMFCS III–V) require hip surveillance X-rays starting at age 1–2 years; migration percentage >30% warrants orthopaedic referral.
keyClue: Spastic bilateral CP, GMFCS III, irritability with nappy changes, limited hip abduction — classic hip migration presentation.
commonExamTrap: Ordering MRI or bone scan before a plain X-ray, which is the first and most informative step.
missedConcept: Hip displacement is the most common orthopaedic complication in spastic CP and requires systematic radiological surveillance.

clinicalStem: A 7-year-old boy with left spastic hemiplegia (GMFCS level I) has been well-managed with physiotherapy and attends mainstream school. He begins having recurrent focal motor seizures affecting his right arm, with occasional secondary generalisation. EEG shows focal left hemisphere sharp waves. MRI brain reveals left periventricular leukomalacia, the underlying cause of his CP. Which antiepileptic agent is most appropriate as initial monotherapy for his focal seizures?
options: Valproic acid (sodium valproate)
Ethosuximide
Oxcarbazepine
Vigabatrin
Clonazepam
explanation: Oxcarbazepine (and its congener carbamazepine) are first-line agents for focal (partial) onset seizures in children. This child has focal motor seizures confirmed by EEG and MRI localisation; focal epilepsy first-line therapy is a sodium channel blocker. Oxcarbazepine is preferred over carbamazepine due to better tolerability and fewer drug interactions.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Valproate is broad-spectrum and often used for generalised epilepsies; it is not the preferred first-line for focal seizures and carries teratogenicity concerns for girls of childbearing potential.
Ethosuximide is indicated exclusively for childhood absence epilepsy (typical absences) and has no efficacy for focal motor seizures.
Correct. Oxcarbazepine is first-line monotherapy for focal seizures in children, acting as a sodium channel blocker.
Vigabatrin is specifically indicated for infantile spasms and TSC-associated spasms; it is not first-line for focal seizures in school-age children due to risk of visual field defects.
Clonazepam is a benzodiazepine used for acute seizure rescue or as adjunctive therapy; it is not recommended as first-line monotherapy for chronic focal epilepsy.
clinicalPearl: Focal seizures (with or without secondary generalisation) in CP are best managed with oxcarbazepine or carbamazepine as first-line monotherapy.
keyClue: Focal motor seizures with EEG localisation in a child with structural left hemisphere pathology — focal epilepsy requiring a sodium channel blocker.
commonExamTrap: Choosing valproate, which is first-line for generalised epilepsies, not for focal epilepsy.
missedConcept: Distinguishing focal from generalised epilepsy syndromes determines first-line drug selection in paediatric epilepsy.

clinicalStem: A 14-month-old boy presents to the emergency department following a first febrile seizure. The seizure was generalised tonic-clonic, lasted 90 seconds, and self-terminated. His temperature on arrival is 38.8°C. He has a three-day history of upper respiratory tract infection. He was born at 36 weeks gestation and has a maternal history of febrile seizures. Neurological examination is normal and he recovers to baseline quickly. His parents ask about the likelihood of another febrile seizure. Which factor in this case MOST significantly increases his risk of recurrence?
options: Duration of the seizure (90 seconds)
Temperature of 38.8°C at presentation
Age at first febrile seizure (14 months)
Generalised tonic-clonic semiology
History of upper respiratory tract infection as the precipitant
explanation: The strongest predictors of febrile seizure recurrence are: young age at first febrile seizure (<18 months), family history of febrile seizures (first-degree relative), low temperature at the time of the seizure, and short duration between fever onset and seizure. Age under 18 months at first febrile seizure is the single most powerful independent predictor of recurrence (approximately 50% recurrence risk).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Duration <15 minutes is a simple febrile seizure; duration does not strongly predict recurrence.
Lower temperatures at onset predict higher recurrence risk; 38.8°C is a moderately high temperature and is not the dominant recurrence predictor here.
Correct. Age <18 months at first febrile seizure is the strongest predictor of recurrence; this child at 14 months fits that criterion.
Generalised seizure semiology does not independently predict recurrence; it is the seizure type classification for simple vs complex.
URTI as precipitant is not a recognised independent predictor of febrile seizure recurrence.
clinicalPearl: The younger the child at first febrile seizure (especially <18 months), the higher the recurrence risk — up to 50% will have at least one more febrile seizure.
keyClue: Age 14 months at first febrile seizure — below the 18-month threshold that independently predicts high recurrence.
commonExamTrap: Choosing low temperature or family history as the primary recurrence predictor; age <18 months is the most powerful single factor.
missedConcept: Risk stratification for febrile seizure recurrence depends primarily on age at onset, family history, and temperature threshold — not seizure duration or semiology.

topic: Dravet syndrome — trigger avoidance and prognosis
subtopic: Long-term seizure control and developmental outcome
clinicalStem: A 3-year-old girl with genetically confirmed Dravet syndrome (SCN1A pathogenic variant) is seen in follow-up. She had her first seizure at 6 months with a prolonged febrile hemiconvulsion. She now has multiple weekly seizures despite trials of valproate and clobazam. Her development has plateaued and she has lost some previously acquired speech. Her parents ask about long-term prognosis and are concerned about a planned family holiday to a hot country. Which of the following statements regarding her prognosis and management is MOST accurate?
options: Her seizures are expected to resolve by school age as in typical febrile seizure syndromes
Sodium channel blockers such as carbamazepine or lamotrigine should be added to optimise seizure control
Hot environments and fever are major seizure triggers; prophylactic antipyretics and temperature management are important safety measures
Developmental regression in Dravet syndrome is always reversible with better seizure control
SUDEP risk in Dravet syndrome is the same as for the general epilepsy population
explanation: Dravet syndrome is associated with hyperthermia-triggered seizures throughout life; hot baths, hot climates, fever, and physical exertion can all precipitate life-threatening seizures. Families must be counselled on temperature avoidance, prompt antipyretic use, and carrying emergency benzodiazepine rescue medication. This is a core safety and prognosis issue in Dravet syndrome management.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Dravet syndrome does not remit; it is a lifelong pharmacoresistant epileptic encephalopathy with persistent seizures and neurodevelopmental impairment.
Sodium channel blockers (carbamazepine, lamotrigine, phenytoin) are CONTRAINDICATED in Dravet syndrome as they worsen seizures by further impairing Nav1.1 function.
Correct. Hyperthermia is a critical trigger in Dravet syndrome; heat exposure during travel, fever, and hot baths must be actively managed.
Developmental regression in Dravet syndrome is often not fully reversible; cognitive and behavioural comorbidities are intrinsic to the syndrome and persist even with improved seizure control.
SUDEP risk in Dravet syndrome is substantially higher than the general epilepsy population, estimated at 0.5–1% per year.
clinicalPearl: Sodium channel blockers are CONTRAINDICATED in Dravet syndrome (SCN1A loss-of-function) — they worsen seizures. Heat is a major trigger requiring active avoidance.
keyClue: SCN1A Dravet syndrome + hot country holiday — hyperthermia as seizure trigger is the key safety concern.
commonExamTrap: Adding carbamazepine or lamotrigine thinking they will help focal-appearing seizures — they paradoxically worsen Dravet syndrome.
missedConcept: In SCN1A Dravet syndrome, sodium channel blockers are contraindicated and hyperthermia management is a lifelong safety priority.

clinicalStem: A 6-year-old boy with known epilepsy is brought to the emergency department by ambulance. He has been having a continuous generalised tonic-clonic seizure for 20 minutes. His parents gave him rectal diazepam (0.5 mg/kg) at 5 minutes as per his care plan. On arrival, he is still seizing, his oxygen saturation is 91% on room air, and he is apnoeic with cyanosis. Intravenous access is established. Which is the MOST appropriate IMMEDIATE next action?
options: Administer IV lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg and prepare IV phenytoin
Administer IV levetiracetam 60 mg/kg as the safest second-line agent
Open the airway, apply high-flow oxygen, and administer IV lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg
Perform rapid sequence intubation immediately before any anticonvulsants
Administer a second dose of rectal diazepam and reassess in 5 minutes
explanation: In status epilepticus, ABCDE management must precede or run simultaneously with anticonvulsant therapy. This child has oxygen desaturation (SpO2 91%) with apnoea and cyanosis — airway and oxygenation take immediate priority. High-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask should be applied immediately. IV lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg is then the correct second-line anticonvulsant (first IV benzodiazepine) given that one prehospital dose of benzodiazepine has already been given.
wrongAnswerExplanations: IV lorazepam is correct but airway management and oxygenation must come first — administering anticonvulsant before addressing hypoxia risks worsening the respiratory compromise.
Levetiracetam is a correct second-line agent but only after airway and oxygen are secured; jumping to it skips the critical resuscitation step.
Correct. Airway opening, high-flow oxygen to correct SpO2 91%, then IV lorazepam as the next anticonvulsant step following the prehospital diazepam.
RSI is not indicated as the immediate first action; most seizures respond to anticonvulsants once oxygenation is restored, and RSI carries its own risks.

A second dose of rectal diazepam is inappropriate — the child already received one benzodiazepine dose, IV access is established, and respiratory compromise requires immediate action, not a repeat PR dose.
clinicalPearl: In status epilepticus, airway and oxygenation take priority before anticonvulsants — hypoxia worsens seizure-induced brain injury. ABCDE first, then benzodiazepines.
keyClue: SpO2 91%, apnoea, cyanosis in an actively seizing child — hypoxia is the immediate threat requiring airway and oxygen before further anticonvulsants.
commonExamTrap: Going straight to IV anticonvulsants without addressing the acute hypoxia and compromised airway.
missedConcept: Status epilepticus management follows ABCDE — respiratory failure from the seizure itself can kill before the seizure does.
redFlagReason: Active status epilepticus with oxygen desaturation (SpO2 91%), apnoea, and cyanosis — immediate airway management and oxygenation are life-saving priorities before further anticonvulsant therapy.

subtopic: Remission and risk of generalised epilepsy
clinicalStem: A 7-year-old girl is diagnosed with childhood absence epilepsy (CAE) after presenting with multiple daily staring episodes lasting 5–10 seconds, confirmed by 3 Hz spike-and-wave on EEG. She is started on ethosuximide and achieves full seizure control within 6 weeks. Her parents ask about long-term prognosis. Which of the following BEST describes the expected outcome for this condition?
options: She will almost certainly develop juvenile myoclonic epilepsy in adolescence and require lifelong antiepileptics
Approximately 60–70% of children with CAE achieve spontaneous remission by adolescence; a minority may develop generalised tonic-clonic seizures
CAE never remits; lifelong antiepileptic therapy is always required
The risk of SUDEP in CAE is similar to that of severe epileptic encephalopathies
Cognitive impairment from repeated absence seizures is permanent and worsens with time regardless of treatment
explanation: Childhood absence epilepsy has a generally favourable prognosis, with remission occurring in approximately 60–70% of cases by adolescence. A minority (10–15%) progress to generalised tonic-clonic seizures, particularly those with persistently difficult-to-control absences or EEG features suggesting a broader epilepsy syndrome. Early seizure control with ethosuximide is associated with better outcomes.
wrongAnswerExplanations: JME is a distinct epilepsy syndrome; CAE does not universally evolve into JME, though a minority do develop broader generalised epilepsy.
Correct. The majority of well-characterised CAE cases remit in adolescence; about 10–15% may have persisting or evolving generalised epilepsy.
CAE can and frequently does remit; it is one of the more benign childhood epilepsy syndromes.
SUDEP risk in CAE is low compared to pharmacoresistant generalised epilepsies; CAE is not associated with significantly elevated SUDEP risk.
Attention and cognitive issues in CAE are often related to seizure frequency and can improve substantially with seizure control; permanent worsening is not the expected course.
clinicalPearl: CAE has a favourable prognosis — approximately 60–70% remit by adolescence; ethosuximide is the first-line treatment of choice for typical absences.
keyClue: Classic 3 Hz spike-and-wave CAE in a 7-year-old girl responding to ethosuximide — benign syndrome with expected remission.
commonExamTrap: Assuming all absence epilepsies are lifelong or inevitably progress to JME.
missedConcept: CAE is one of the few childhood epilepsy syndromes with a true remission rate in the majority of cases.

clinicalStem: A 3-year-old boy with Sturge-Weber syndrome (right facial port-wine stain involving V1 dermatome, leptomeningeal angiomatosis on MRI) is followed in a multidisciplinary clinic. He has been seizure-free on oxcarbazepine for 18 months. At a routine clinic visit, his mother mentions he has been rubbing his right eye frequently and appears bothered by light. Examination shows a hazy right cornea and the right eye appears slightly larger than the left. Which complication is MOST likely responsible for these findings?
options: Cortical visual impairment from occipital leptomeningeal angiomatosis
Amblyopia secondary to the facial port-wine stain obscuring vision
Congenital glaucoma (buphthalmos) due to ipsilateral choroidal haemangioma
Optic neuritis from chronic anti-epileptic drug toxicity
Conjunctivitis secondary to frequent eye rubbing
explanation: Sturge-Weber syndrome affecting the V1 (ophthalmic) dermatomal distribution is associated with ipsilateral choroidal haemangioma and episcleral haemangioma, both of which obstruct aqueous drainage and cause elevated intraocular pressure. Buphthalmos (enlarged globe), corneal clouding, photophobia, and eye rubbing in an infant/toddler are classic signs of congenital glaucoma, which is a well-recognised and serious complication of Sturge-Weber syndrome.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Cortical visual impairment from occipital involvement causes visual field defects and cortical blindness — not buphthalmos or corneal haziness.
Port-wine stain alone does not obstruct the visual axis; amblyopia in SWS is usually caused by untreated glaucoma rather than the skin lesion itself.
Correct. Congenital glaucoma (buphthalmos) from elevated IOP due to choroidal haemangioma is the classic ocular complication of Sturge-Weber with V1 involvement.
Oxcarbazepine does not cause optic neuritis; this presentation is structural, not inflammatory or toxic.
Conjunctivitis does not cause corneal haziness or globe enlargement; this is a structural ocular emergency.
clinicalPearl: Sturge-Weber syndrome with V1 port-wine stain requires lifelong ophthalmological surveillance for glaucoma; buphthalmos (globe enlargement) and corneal haziness are early signs of elevated IOP.
keyClue: Hazy cornea + enlarged globe (buphthalmos) + photophobia in a child with Sturge-Weber V1 involvement — congenital glaucoma.
commonExamTrap: Attributing the eye symptoms to neurological visual impairment rather than the structural glaucomatous complication.
missedConcept: V1 Sturge-Weber syndrome mandates ophthalmology surveillance for glaucoma from birth — it is the most important ocular complication.

clinicalStem: An 8-year-old boy with Lennox-Gastaut syndrome (LGS) is reviewed in the paediatric neurology clinic. He has multiple daily seizure types including tonic, atonic (drop attacks), and absence seizures with slow spike-wave on EEG. He has moderate intellectual disability. Despite trials of valproate, lamotrigine, and clobazam, he continues to have five to ten drop attacks daily, resulting in multiple head injuries. Examination reveals facial bruising and a healing scalp laceration. His parents are struggling to cope. Which intervention is MOST likely to reduce his drop attacks and head injury burden?
options: Increase valproate to the maximum tolerated dose as monotherapy
Refer for vagus nerve stimulation (VNS) or corpus callosotomy to specifically target drop attacks
Add ethosuximide as fourth-line therapy for the absence component
Start prednisolone pulse therapy as for infantile spasms
Initiate ketogenic diet and prescribe a protective helmet
explanation: In LGS, drop attacks (atonic seizures) causing injury are a major management challenge. The ketogenic diet has Level 1 evidence for reducing seizure frequency by >50% in pharmacoresistant LGS. A protective helmet is an important adjunctive safety measure to prevent head injuries from drop attacks while dietary and pharmacological optimisation continues. This combined approach addresses both seizure reduction and injury prevention.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Maximising valproate as monotherapy after multiple drug failures is unlikely to achieve meaningful additional benefit and increases toxicity risk.

VNS and corpus callosotomy are valid options in LGS; corpus callosotomy specifically targets drop attacks with good evidence, and VNS reduces overall seizure burden. However, in a child already on three medications with active injury risk, the ketogenic diet plus helmet offers both seizure reduction and immediate safety benefit in a less invasive way. Both VNS/callosotomy and the diet are valid — this question targets the combination of seizure reduction and injury prevention.
Ethosuximide targets only typical absence seizures; it has no efficacy for tonic or atonic seizures in LGS.
Prednisolone is used for infantile spasms (West syndrome) and is not an established therapy for LGS after the infantile period.
Correct. The ketogenic diet has robust evidence in pharmacoresistant LGS; combined with a protective helmet, it addresses both seizure frequency and the critical head injury burden.
clinicalPearl: In LGS with frequent drop attacks causing injury, the ketogenic diet reduces seizure burden and a protective helmet is an essential immediate safety intervention — corpus callosotomy specifically targets drop attacks when diet fails.
keyClue: Multiple daily drop attacks with repeated head injuries in LGS despite three drug trials — non-pharmacological seizure reduction (ketogenic diet) plus injury prevention (helmet).
commonExamTrap: Continuing to add further antiepileptic drugs without considering non-pharmacological interventions in established pharmacoresistant LGS.
missedConcept: The ketogenic diet has strong evidence in LGS; protective helmets address the immediate injury risk from drop attacks.

clinicalStem: A 5-year-old girl with severe spastic quadriplegic cerebral palsy (GMFCS level V) has a weight consistently below the 3rd centile and has dropped two centile lines over the past year. Her parents report that mealtimes take over 45 minutes and she frequently coughs during feeds. She has had three episodes of aspiration pneumonia in the past 12 months. Clinical examination reveals marked wasting and poor growth velocity. Modified barium swallow confirms pharyngeal dysphagia with silent aspiration. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate next management step?
options: Fortify her current oral diet with high-calorie supplements and reassess in 6 months
Refer for gastrostomy (PEG) placement to ensure safe enteral nutrition and prevent further aspiration
Commence nasogastric tube feeding on a long-term basis as an alternative to gastrostomy
Restrict oral feeds entirely and rely on intravenous nutrition pending dietitian review
Increase oral feeding frequency to 8 times daily to improve calorie intake
explanation: Severe dysphagia with confirmed silent aspiration, recurrent aspiration pneumonia (three episodes in 12 months), and progressive growth failure in a child with GMFCS V CP are definitive indications for gastrostomy placement. PEG/gastrostomy is the gold standard for long-term enteral nutrition in CP children who cannot safely or adequately feed orally. It significantly reduces aspiration pneumonia episodes and improves growth, quality of life, and caregiver burden.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Fortifying oral feeds is appropriate for mild to moderate feeding difficulties; it is contraindicated here given confirmed silent aspiration and recurrent aspiration pneumonia.
Correct. Gastrostomy is indicated with confirmed aspiration, recurrent pneumonia, and growth failure — it is the safest long-term enteral feeding route in this context.
Long-term nasogastric tube is not the preferred approach; it is uncomfortable, dislodges frequently, and does not prevent aspiration as effectively as gastrostomy in CP.
IV nutrition has no role in chronic nutritional management of CP; it is reserved for acute post-operative or critical illness settings.
Increasing oral feeding frequency does not address the aspiration risk — more feeds means more aspiration events, worsening her pneumonia risk.
clinicalPearl: Recurrent aspiration pneumonia (>2–3 episodes per year) with confirmed silent aspiration on modified barium swallow is a strong indication for gastrostomy in children with severe CP.
keyClue: Three aspiration pneumonias in 12 months + silent aspiration on modified barium swallow + growth failure in GMFCS V CP — definitive gastrostomy indication.
commonExamTrap: Choosing long-term nasogastric feeds over gastrostomy for a child with confirmed chronic aspiration and growth failure.
missedConcept: Gastrostomy is the standard of care for long-term enteral nutrition in GMFCS IV–V CP with dysphagia; it reduces aspiration and improves growth outcomes.

clinicalStem: A 10-month-old boy was diagnosed with West syndrome at 5 months of age following onset of infantile spasms. Brain MRI showed cortical dysplasia. He was treated with vigabatrin and ACTH, achieving cessation of spasms after 4 weeks. He is now reviewed at 10 months; he has no current spasms but his EEG, previously showing hypsarrhythmia, now shows slow background with multifocal spike-wave discharges. His developmental milestones are significantly delayed and he has started having brief tonic seizures. Which of the following statements regarding his prognosis is MOST accurate?
options: Resolution of hypsarrhythmia on EEG confirms long-term neurodevelopmental recovery
The appearance of tonic seizures and multifocal spike-wave after West syndrome suggests evolution to Lennox-Gastaut syndrome
Cortical dysplasia as the underlying aetiology confers the same prognosis as cryptogenic West syndrome
Vigabatrin treatment guarantees a normal developmental outcome if spasms are suppressed early
Transition from infantile spasms to Lennox-Gastaut syndrome only occurs if ACTH was not used
explanation: West syndrome with a structural/symptomatic aetiology (such as cortical dysplasia) has a poor prognosis. Transition to Lennox-Gastaut syndrome occurs in approximately 30–50% of cases, characterised by the emergence of multiple seizure types (particularly tonic and atonic), diffuse slow spike-wave on EEG (replacing hypsarrhythmia), and continued neurodevelopmental regression. This patient's clinical and EEG features are consistent with evolving LGS.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Disappearance of hypsarrhythmia does not guarantee developmental recovery, especially in structural West syndrome; it may simply reflect evolution to a different but still abnormal EEG pattern.
Correct. Tonic seizures + multifocal slow spike-wave after West syndrome = emerging LGS, especially in a child with structural aetiology and continued developmental delay.
Structural aetiology (cortical dysplasia) confers a significantly worse prognosis than cryptogenic/unknown West syndrome; this distinction is critical for prognostic counselling.
Early suppression of spasms improves prognosis but does not guarantee normal development in structural West syndrome; the underlying brain pathology continues to impact development.
Transition to LGS is determined by aetiology and underlying brain pathology, not by whether ACTH was used; it is not preventable by specific choice of ACTH vs vigabatrin alone.
clinicalPearl: West syndrome with structural aetiology (e.g., cortical dysplasia) has a poor prognosis; approximately 30–50% evolve into Lennox-Gastaut syndrome with tonic seizures and slow spike-wave EEG.
keyClue: Post-infantile-spasms tonic seizures + multifocal slow spike-wave (replacing hypsarrhythmia) in a child with cortical dysplasia — West syndrome evolving to LGS.
commonExamTrap: Assuming that cessation of infantile spasms and resolution of hypsarrhythmia indicate a good long-term outcome regardless of underlying aetiology.
missedConcept: Structural aetiology in West syndrome predicts poor prognosis and high risk of LGS evolution; aetiology is the dominant prognostic factor, not treatment response alone.
